title: How to Work with Psychedelic Insights About Aging and Time
body:
1. The Moment Psychedelic Time Stretched for Me

I still remember the moment as if it were yesterday, though it happened years ago. Sitting alone in a quiet room, the weight of age and time pressing on me like a thick fog, I took a moderate dose of psilocybin mushrooms. What unfolded was not the typical kaleidoscope of colors or the symphony of laughter that many talk about. Instead, I felt time itself stretch, elongate, and fold in on itself. Minutes felt like hours; hours melted into memories from decades ago. I saw my younger self sitting beside me, and my future self waiting patiently in the distance. In that timeless state, the usual anxiety about aging - the creeping sense of loss, the relentless ticking clock - dissolved into a vast ocean of acceptance.

That experience changed how I relate to time and aging to this day. I can’t count the number of people I’ve talked to who say the same thing: psychedelics make you feel like time is flexible, malleable, sometimes even irrelevant. And yet, when we come down, the usual worries about getting older return, often with a vengeance. How do we hold onto those insights from the psychedelic experience when the world insists on its linear march? Can we reshape our relationship with aging and time beyond the trip?

These questions kept gnawing at me, and over the years, I’ve found ways to work with psychedelic insights about aging and time - not just in the moments of altered consciousness but in the quiet hours of everyday life. I’ve seen how these insights can open doors to deeper understanding, healing, and a more compassionate acceptance of what it means to grow older. But it’s never simple, nor is it automatic. It requires intention, patience, and tools that bridge the vastness of psychedelic experience with the grounded reality of our human bodies and lives.

Before I get ahead of myself, let me start by unpacking what’s really going on when psychedelics change our experience of time and self. This is where science meets the mystery, and understanding that helps us hold the experience rather than chase it. If you’ve ever felt that your trips bring you closer to some timeless truth about your own life and aging, this article is for you. Let’s explore how to work with those insights so they can become part of your everyday wisdom, not just fleeting moments of awe.

2. What Happens When Psychedelics Bend Time and Self

The way psychedelics shift our perception of time and aging is deeply rooted in how they affect the brain’s networks. At a neuroscience level, substances like psilocybin and LSD decrease activity in the brain’s default mode network (DMN), a collection of regions responsible for our sense of self, autobiographical memory, and the continuous narrative we tell ourselves. When the DMN quiets down, the usual boundaries of the "self" and the linear experience of time loosen, allowing for what researchers call "ego dissolution" and altered time perception.

Rick Strassman, the “God Molecule” researcher, along with figures like Robin Carhart-Harris, have shown that psychedelics temporarily disrupt the usual communication patterns in the brain, causing a more globally interconnected state. This state allows for memories, sensations, and emotions from various times in one’s life to co-exist simultaneously. That’s why you might suddenly relive a vivid childhood moment or feel a future version of yourself in the same experience. The brain isn’t processing time in a linear way but rather in a more whole-person, multi-layered manner.

Neuroscience also suggests that this disruption frees us from the usual grip of time-bound anxieties. The fear of aging, looming death, or missed opportunities often depends on the rigid clock of our mind’s narrative. When that clock stops ticking so predictably, it opens a window for new perspectives. You can witness your life unfolding from a place that doesn’t judge the passing years but rather appreciates the flow of existence as a whole.

Stanislav Grof’s early work with LSD therapy emphasized this phenomenology of time. He described experiences where patients relived not just their own past but also transpersonal or collective memories, suggesting that our usual sense of time is a construct rather than an absolute reality. In modern terms, we can think of this as a "time dilation" or "time collapse" effect, where past, present, and future merge in the psychedelic mindspace.

Understanding these mechanisms is crucial because it explains why psychedelic insights about aging don’t just feel deep - they’re a glimpse into a different operating system of the brain. The challenge then becomes: how do we translate this altered state experience back into our everyday lives, where time is linear, and bodies age relentlessly? That’s the bridge we have to build, with awareness and practical methods.

3. What the Science Says About Psychedelics, Aging, and Time

There’s a growing body of research examining how psychedelics affect our sense of time and attitudes toward aging. Johns Hopkins University is among the institutions leading this charge. In a landmark study, Roland Griffiths and colleagues found that a single high dose of psilocybin induced mystical-type experiences with lasting positive changes in attitudes, mood, and behavior, including decreased fear of death. This is relevant because fear of aging is often intertwined with fear of mortality.

Another important study by Robin Carhart-Harris’s group at Imperial College London used fMRI scans to observe brain activity under psilocybin. They confirmed that the decrease in DMN activity correlates with ego dissolution and altered time perception. Participants reported feeling “timelessness” or a sense that time was “non-linear.” This has deep implications for how we experience aging, which is usually framed as a linear progression toward decline and death.

In terms of aging itself, a fascinating pilot study from the Usona Institute explores how psychedelics might alleviate symptoms common in older adults, such as anxiety and depression, which often stem from fears about aging and mortality. While the sample sizes are still small, early results suggest that psychedelic therapy can improve emotional regulation and shift perspectives on the passage of time.

There is also an intriguing crossover with studies on narrative psychology. Psychedelic experiences often disrupt habitual life stories, allowing patients to rewrite their personal narratives with more self-compassion and less fear of aging. Michael Pollan’s book How to Change Your Mind highlights many of these findings, describing cases where people who struggled with midlife crises or end-of-life anxiety found relief through psychedelic therapy.

These findings align with Bessel van der Kolk’s work on trauma and the body. In The Body Keeps the Score, van der Kolk emphasizes how traumatic memories and fears are stored somatically, affecting our sense of time and self. Psychedelic therapy can loosen these somatic hold points, giving us a chance to rewrite our relationship with aging.

So the science supports what many have experienced firsthand: psychedelics don’t just alter time perception temporarily; they enable a shift in how we emotionally and cognitively engage with aging. This is why the psychedelic space is fertile ground for healing age-related fears and stress - but only if the insights are treated thoughtfully, with care and integration.

4. How to Work Psychedelic Insights About Aging Into Your Life

If you’re reading this, you might have already had psychedelic experiences that challenged your relationship with aging and time. Or you might be curious about how to make those insights last beyond the session. Here are some ways you can work with those insights in a practical, grounded way.

First, journaling is key. After your experience, give yourself time to write freely about what you remember feeling around time and aging. Don’t censor yourself; let the feelings and thoughts flow. I keep a Journaling Notebook Leather Bound close by specifically for this. Writing helps you externalize the psychedelic insights and makes them easier to revisit and unpack later. You might find recurring themes around acceptance, loss, or timelessness emerging, which can guide your integration work.

Second, mindfulness and meditation practices are invaluable. Psychedelic experiences often touch on timelessness, but meditation helps cultivate that state gently in your daily life. Try spending a few minutes focusing on your breath or sensations in your body, noticing how time feels in those moments. This practice stabilizes the flexible time perception you experienced on the mushrooms into a resource you can draw on when stress about aging creeps in.

Third, consider talking to a therapist or integration coach familiar with psychedelic work - someone who can help you process the sometimes challenging emotions that arise about aging. A skilled guide can help you explore how your cultural beliefs, family history, or past trauma influence your fears and expectations around getting older, helping you rewrite those stories.

Building a ritual that honors the passage of time also helps. This might mean setting intentional moments to check in with yourself - monthly reflections on what aging means to you, or seasonal ceremonies marking your growth. These moments ground the psychedelic insight in your body and mind, reminding you that time’s flow is natural and your life is unfolding with meaning.

Lastly, engage your body. Movement practices like yoga, tai chi, or even simple walks in nature help connect you back to the physical experience of time passing, from the heartbeat to the changing seasons. This somatic awareness supports integration by bridging the gap between psychedelic states and everyday life, helping you embody new ways of relating to your aging self.

5. The Bumps on the Road - Challenges You Might Face

Working with psychedelic insights about aging and time isn’t always smooth sailing. One common trap is the tendency to chase the psychedelic experience itself, hoping each trip will bring you closer to some perfect understanding or relief from aging fears. This can backfire, creating frustration or disappointment when the insights don’t persist or when your anxiety returns.

Another challenge is the emotional rawness that often arises. Psychedelic sessions can bring buried fears to the surface - grief over lost youth, regret about past choices, or anxiety about not having enough time left. These feelings can feel overwhelming, especially if you don’t have the tools or support to work through them. I’ve seen people shut down or push away these emotions, which only prolongs suffering.

Integration fatigue is real too. After a session, you might feel energized and hopeful, but integration requires ongoing commitment. When the weeks pass, daily life - work, family, routines - pulls your attention away from integration practices. Without regular reflection, journaling, or mindfulness, the insights fade, and old habits return. This cycle can lead to discouragement or a sense that the psychedelic experience was “just a trip” without lasting benefit.

Sometimes, the cultural messages we receive about aging get in the way. Aging is often framed negatively in Western culture - as decline, loss, or something to be feared and avoided. Even if your psychedelic experience showed a different view, you might find yourself unconsciously absorbing societal messages that contradict your new understanding. This can create internal tension and confusion.

Practical advice for these challenges: don’t isolate. Find a community or support system where you can share your experiences and struggles openly. Whether that’s a psychedelic integration group, trusted friends, or a therapist, connection helps normalize your journey. Also, be patient and gentle with yourself. Integration is a marathon, not a sprint. When difficult emotions arise, try to meet them with curiosity rather than resistance.

And finally, keep journaling - even if it feels repetitive or slow. Sometimes the most valuable insights come weeks or months after the experience, when you least expect them. Writing helps you track your evolving relationship with time and aging, revealing patterns and breakthroughs you might miss otherwise.

6. Weaving Psychedelic Wisdom Into Everyday Aging

Integration isn’t about fitting psychedelic insights into your life as a one-time event. It’s about letting those insights gently reshape your ongoing experience of aging and time. You’re not trying to hold onto a fleeting mystical moment but to cultivate a new orientation toward your life’s unfolding.

One way I do this is by celebrating milestones differently. Instead of dreading birthdays or anniversaries as reminders of getting older, I try to treat them as markers of growth and resilience. After psychedelic therapy, I saw age as a collection of stories, lessons, and moments that form a rich human experience. That mindset shifts how I approach getting older - from resistance to curiosity.

Slow down. Psychedelic time teaches you that rushing through life is a kind of illusion. I try to practice intentional slowness in daily life - savoring meals, walking without distraction, listening deeply to others. This slows the felt passage of time and makes each moment fuller. It’s a small act but radically changes how I feel about the years ahead.

Also, I keep revisiting my journals regularly. They are like a map of my lifelong relationship with time and aging. Sometimes I’ll read passages from years ago and see how my understanding has evolved. This practice keeps the psychedelic insights alive in a dynamic way, not locked as some static truth. For me, integration is an ongoing conversation between my evolving self and the wisdom the mushrooms have offered.

Physical care becomes more compassionate too. Psychedelics help me feel my body as a living, breathing entity rather than a machine that’s breaking down. That changes my approach to aging: instead of punishment or neglect, I seek grace and kindness in movement, nutrition, and rest. This is a practical acknowledgment of the body’s inevitable changes while honoring its vitality.

Finally, I embrace uncertainty. Psychedelic time is often non-linear, unpredictable, and mysterious. Life’s later years can feel the same way. I try not to grip too tightly to plans or ideas about how things should be. Instead, I welcome the unknown with openness, trusting that whatever time I have is rich with possibility, even if it’s different from youth.

7. A New Way to Feel Time and Aging - An Invitation

Here’s a thought I want to leave with you. What if aging isn’t just about getting older or closer to death but about deepening into something timeless already present in you? Psychedelic experiences can reveal this, showing us that beneath the line of years is a vast expanse where self, time, and existence mingle freely.

We tend to live as if time is a thief, stealing youth, health, and dreams. Yet the psychedelic mind often hints at a different truth: time is a teacher, a storyteller, a river carrying us toward deeper understanding. The wrinkles and gray hairs become not signs of loss but badges of courage - proof that we have navigated life’s currents, sometimes turbulent, sometimes calm.

This way of feeling time and aging is subtle and can’t be forced. It grows in silence, in the small moments when you remember your psychedelic insights and choose to see time not as an enemy but a companion. It invites you to listen to your body’s wisdom, to forgive past regrets, and to open to future mysteries with quiet wonder.

In my own journey, I’ve come to realize that psychedelic insights about time and aging are gifts meant to be lived into, not locked away as secrets or ideals. They challenge us to live fully now, to honor what has come before, and to meet what’s ahead with a gentle heart. Holding that in mind changes everything - from how we care for ourselves to how we relate to others.

If you carry these insights with you, you’re participating in a new way of aging - not denying the passage of years but embracing the fullness of time itself. This is a conversation worth having every day, with patience, with courage, and with the open heart that psychedelics so often help us find.
